_comment: Gold-set template (v2) — the canonical format read by src/pipeline/calibration.py (load_gold_set). Replace the skeleton items with ~25-40 REAL answers per category from a proxy run (pull straight from docs/answers.md), hand-labeled by a human (never by a model — independence is the point). Run: python -m src.pipeline.calibration
_labeling_rules: present: true/false — is the brand mentioned at all in the answer?
prominence: one of recommended_first | mid_pack | buried | also_ran | absent. Relative within the answer (who is named first vs. buried). absent if and only if present=false.
framing: one of positive | neutral | negative. NO 'n/a' — absent brands get 'neutral' (matches the judge's default).
labels: include one entry per brand for the client AND every competitor listed on the item, even when absent.
fact_sheet: inline text (paste the relevant facts) or null. Only items WITH a fact sheet count toward flag precision/recall.

expected_flags (v2): the human's TYPED list of the real client errors the answer makes — [{type, severity, note}]. type from {wrong_pricing, missing_or_invented_feature, competitor_confusion, identity, stale}; severity from {high, med, low}; note is free text (not scored). An empty list [] means 'checked, accurate'. Provide expected_flags on every item you label — it drives flag precision/recall. (Legacy items with only the old expect_accuracy_flags boolean still load.)

fact_sheet_candidates (v2, optional): claims the answer makes that the fact sheet does NOT cover, so the judge must NOT flag them. Listing them documents the over-flagging trap; a judge flag landing on a candidate is a precision error.
engine / category (v2, optional): set these so the report breaks agreement down per engine and per category — the slices reveal whether accuracy is uniform or concentrated.
Freeze the file once judge testing starts; split into a tune subset and a held-out subset, report only on held-out, and draw any judge-prompt few-shot examples ONLY from the tune subset.

items._notes: Skeleton item 1 — client mentioned WITH a wrong claim. List each real error in expected_flags (typed).
items.answer: {paste the FULL raw engine answer here}
fact_sheet_candidates: {any claim the answer makes that the sheet does not cover — the judge must NOT flag it}

items._notes: Skeleton item 2 — a 'losing query': client absent, no fact sheet (accuracy not assessed). expected_flags is [] (accurate / nothing to flag).
items.answer: {paste the FULL raw engine answer here}
